heroOverview: Suberosis is a form of hypersensitivity pneumonitis caused by repeated inhalation of fungal spores — primarily Penicillium glabrum and Chrysonilia sitophila — that colonise stored cork bark and cork dust in the cork processing industry. It is classified under ICD-10 J67.3 and is named for Quercus suber, the cork oak tree native to Portugal, Spain, Algeria, and Morocco, which together produce over 80% of the world's commercial cork. Workers who strip, grade, boil, cut, or grind cork bark are at the highest occupational risk. The immune-mediated alveolitis that suberosis produces is identical in mechanism to other forms of extrinsic allergic alveolitis, but the antigen source — cork dust fungi — is specific to this trade. Without exposure reduction, repeated episodes lead to progressive pulmonary fibrosis and irreversible lung function loss in a significant minority of affected workers.

keyStats.prevalence: Affects approximately 8–15% of heavily exposed cork workers in endemic regions; prevalence depends strongly on dust control practices and ventilation in the processing facility
keyStats.demographics: Primarily affects adults aged 20–55 working in cork processing; Portugal accounts for the largest number of reported cases given its dominant position in global cork production
keyStats.avgAge: Most cases are diagnosed in adults aged 30–50 after 5–15 years of cumulative exposure; onset after acute high-level exposure can occur earlier
keyStats.globalCases: Concentrated in Mediterranean cork-producing regions; exact global case counts are not reported, but the condition is the most common occupational lung disease in the Portuguese cork industry

definition: Suberosis (ICD-10: J67.3) is an extrinsic allergic alveolitis — also known as hypersensitivity pneumonitis — caused by sensitisation to antigens derived from Penicillium glabrum (formerly Penicillium frequentans) and related mould species, principally Chrysonilia sitophila, that proliferate in moist, stacked cork bark awaiting or undergoing industrial processing. The name derives from Quercus suber, the cork oak. The pathophysiological mechanism involves repeated inhalation of fungal conidia triggering both a type III (immune complex–mediated) and a type IV (cell-mediated, delayed-type hypersensitivity) immunological reaction in the alveoli and distal bronchioles. Sensitised workers' alveolar macrophages and T-lymphocytes respond to re-exposure by producing a granulomatous and lymphocytic alveolitis that impairs gas exchange and, with repeated or continuous antigen challenge, transitions from reversible cellular inflammation to irreversible interstitial fibrosis and honeycombing. Suberosis falls within the broader spectrum of organic dust hypersensitivity pneumonitides alongside farmer's lung (Saccharopolyspora rectivirgula from mouldy hay), bird fancier's lung (avian serum proteins and bloom), bagassosis (Thermophilic actinomycetes from sugar cane), and mushroom worker's lung. It is classified under pulmonology and occupational medicine, and its diagnosis rests on the integration of occupational history, respiratory function testing, high-resolution computed tomography, specific antigen serology, and occasionally bronchoalveolar lavage or surgical lung biopsy.

typesClassification.description: Develops within 4–8 hours of a heavy inhalation exposure; presents with fever, chills, cough, dyspnoea, and myalgia resembling influenza. Symptoms typically peak at 12–24 hours and resolve within 24–72 hours when the worker leaves the exposure environment. Recurrent acute episodes cause cumulative lung damage.

Results from prolonged moderate-level exposure over weeks to months; presents as a gradual progressive cough, fatigue, and effort dyspnoea without the discrete fever episodes of acute disease. May be misattributed to viral illness or asthma before the occupational link is recognised.

The most serious form, resulting from years of unrecognised or uncontrolled exposure; the alveolitis transitions to interstitial fibrosis with a progressive decline in lung volumes, diffusing capacity, and exercise tolerance. Fibrosis may continue to advance even after exposure has ceased.

primarySymptoms: Progressive dyspnoea on exertion — the earliest and most consistent symptom, beginning with breathlessness only during strenuous activity and worsening over months to affect daily tasks.
Dry or minimally productive cough that is persistent and may worsen after work shifts in the cork processing environment.
Fever and chills 4–8 hours after heavy exposure in the acute form, resolving within 24–72 hours when the worker goes home, then recurring with the next exposure.
Myalgia and arthralgia during febrile episodes in acute suberosis, giving the presentation a flu-like character that frequently leads to misdiagnosis as a viral illness.
Fatigue and malaise that is out of proportion to the degree of respiratory symptoms, particularly in the subacute form.
Bibasal inspiratory crackles (fine crackles) heard on lung auscultation, reflecting alveolar inflammation or fibrosis in the lower lung zones.
Chest tightness or a sensation of chest heaviness, particularly at the end of a working shift with heavy cork dust exposure.
Weight loss in advanced or chronic disease, reflecting the energy cost of increased respiratory work and systemic inflammation.
Hypoxaemia at rest (low blood oxygen saturation) in advanced disease, identifiable by oxygen saturation measurement and confirmed by arterial blood gas analysis.
Finger clubbing in a minority of patients with established fibrosis, though this feature is less frequent in suberosis than in idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis.
earlyWarningSigns: Symptoms of flu — fever, chills, muscle aching — that consistently appear 4–8 hours after starting a shift in the cork plant and resolve by the weekend or holiday.
A persistent cough that is worse at work and improves on days away from the cork processing environment.
Gradual increase in breathlessness on physical effort over several months in a cork industry worker.
A colleague at the same workstation developing similar respiratory symptoms.
emergencySigns: Severe breathlessness at rest or inability to complete a sentence, indicating severe acute alveolitis or acute respiratory failure.
Oxygen saturation below 90% on pulse oximetry, which requires emergency supplemental oxygen and hospital assessment.
High fever (above 39°C) with rapidly worsening breathlessness that does not resolve after 24 hours away from exposure, suggesting an unusually severe acute episode or concurrent pneumonia.
Central cyanosis (blue lips or fingertips) indicating critical hypoxaemia.
Acute decompensation in a patient with known chronic suberosis — sudden dramatic worsening of dyspnoea in a previously stable patient.

causes.cause: Inhalation of Penicillium glabrum spores from cork dust
causes.description: Penicillium glabrum (formerly P. frequentans) is the dominant sensitising antigen in suberosis; it colonises stored cork bark at high concentrations, particularly when the bark is damp. Cork stripping, grinding, and boiling operations generate massive spore-laden dust clouds, and repeated inhalation of these spores triggers progressive alveolar sensitisation in susceptible workers.

A second mould species that co-colonises cork in processing facilities; its conidia contribute independently to antigen load and have been identified as a cause of hypersensitivity pneumonitis in cork workers in controlled antigen studies. Sensitisation to both Penicillium and Chrysonilia is common in affected workers.

causes.cause: High-level or unventilated cork processing environments
causes.description: The concentration of fungal spores in poorly ventilated cork processing buildings can reach levels thousands of times higher than safe occupational exposure limits. Poor building ventilation, inadequate dust extraction, and absence of respiratory protective equipment are the key environmental determinants of dose and disease onset.

Not all cork workers who are equally exposed develop suberosis; certain HLA haplotypes (including HLA-DR3 and HLA-B8) and polymorphisms in immunoregulatory genes are associated with a higher risk of hypersensitivity pneumonitis across antigen types. This accounts for the 8–15% disease rate even in heavily exposed groups.

causes.cause: Cumulative exposure duration without adequate protection
causes.description: Both the intensity and duration of cork dust inhalation are dose-dependent risk factors; workers with 10 or more years of unprotected exposure to high dust environments have the highest rates of sensitisation and progression to fibrosis, and the relationship between cumulative exposure and fibrotic disease is well documented in Portuguese cohort studies.

riskFactors.factor: Working in cork processing without respiratory protection
riskFactors.description: Stripping, grinding, and cutting cork without a fitted respirator or in a facility without dust extraction delivers the highest fungal spore doses; mandatory use of N95 or equivalent respirators substantially reduces exposure and disease risk in controlled occupational studies.

riskFactors.factor: Duration of employment in the cork industry
riskFactors.description: Cumulative years of employment in cork processing directly correlates with sensitisation rates and the severity of lung function impairment; workers with over 10 years of exposure show significantly higher rates of pulmonary fibrosis than those with fewer years, even after controlling for intensity.

riskFactors.factor: Working in an inadequately ventilated facility
riskFactors.description: Enclosed cork warehouses and processing halls without powered ventilation or dust extraction concentrate fungal spores to levels far exceeding occupational health limits; facility-level engineering controls are the most effective single intervention.

Certain HLA haplotypes linked to exaggerated immune responses to inhaled antigens increase the risk of developing hypersensitivity pneumonitis; this is consistent across all forms of extrinsic allergic alveolitis and explains why sensitisation is not universal in a heavily exposed workforce.

riskFactors.factor: Continued exposure after the first symptomatic episode
riskFactors.description: Returning to the same high-exposure environment without additional protection after an acute episode is the main modifiable driver of progression to fibrosis; a first acute episode is an opportunity to intervene before irreversible lung damage occurs.

Paradoxically, active smoking is associated with a reduced risk of sensitisation (possibly through immune suppression of the alveolar response) but worsens the functional impact of established disease; smoking cessation improves overall lung health in affected workers even if it does not reduce sensitisation risk in active smokers.

affectedDemographics: Cork processing workers in Portugal, Spain, Algeria, and Morocco — the four main cork-producing countries
Workers in cork stopper manufacturing, cork agglomerate, and insulation board production
Male workers predominantly, reflecting the male-majority demographics of the Portuguese cork industry workforce
Workers aged 20–55 with at least 2–5 years of occupational exposure
Individuals with certain HLA haplotypes conferring immunological susceptibility to extrinsic allergic alveolitis
Cork warehouse workers who manage bulk stored cork bark under damp conditions
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosing suberosis requires integrating the occupational history, clinical presentation, lung function tests, radiology, and antigen serology. The key diagnostic clue is the temporal relationship between cork dust exposure and symptoms: fever and breathlessness that recur within hours of each work shift and resolve over weekends or holidays are strongly suggestive. Spirometry typically shows a restrictive pattern (reduced forced vital capacity with preserved FEV1/FVC ratio), and the diffusing capacity for carbon monoxide (DLCO) is reduced even before spirometric restriction becomes apparent. High-resolution CT (HRCT) of the thorax shows bilateral ground-glass opacification, centrilobular nodules, and mosaic attenuation in acute and subacute disease; established fibrosis produces honeycombing, traction bronchiectasis, and volume loss, predominantly in the upper and mid-zones in hypersensitivity pneumonitis (distinct from the lower-zone predominance of idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis). Serum precipitins (IgG antibodies) against Penicillium glabrum and Chrysonilia sitophila antigens are present in 50–70% of affected workers, though positive serology alone indicates sensitisation rather than active disease, and negative serology does not exclude the diagnosis. Inhalation challenge testing — exposing the patient to the suspected antigen under monitored conditions — is diagnostic but is mainly reserved for uncertain cases. Bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL) in active disease shows lymphocytosis with a CD4:CD8 ratio below 1, consistent with a hypersensitivity pneumonitis pattern. Surgical lung biopsy, when performed, shows poorly formed granulomas, lymphocytic alveolitis, and peribronchiolar fibrosis. The main differentials are idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis, sarcoidosis, other hypersensitivity pneumonitides (farmer's lung, bird fancier's lung), and occupational asthma.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The most sensitive imaging test for detecting alveolitis and fibrosis; identifies characteristic patterns of hypersensitivity pneumonitis including ground-glass opacification, centrilobular nodules, mosaic attenuation, and — in advanced disease — honeycombing and traction bronchiectasis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A CT scan of the chest taking approximately 5–10 minutes; no injection is needed for standard HRCT. Results are reported by a radiologist, and interpretation requires knowledge of the occupational context.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Documents the restrictive lung function pattern and reduced gas transfer that characterise hypersensitivity pneumonitis, and monitors disease progression over time.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Spirometry involves breathing forcefully into a mouthpiece connected to a flow sensor; three to five efforts are required. DLCO involves inhaling a small amount of carbon monoxide, holding the breath for 10 seconds, and exhaling. Results are available immediately.

diagnosticTests.test: Serum precipitins (IgG antibodies) against cork dust antigens
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects specific IgG antibodies against Penicillium glabrum and Chrysonilia sitophila antigens as evidence of immunological sensitisation, supporting the diagnosis when combined with exposure history and clinical features.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw sent to a specialist immunology laboratory; results may take several days. Positive serology in an asymptomatic worker does not confirm disease — clinical correlation is essential.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms alveolar lymphocytosis with a CD4:CD8 ratio below 1, a pattern consistent with hypersensitivity pneumonitis, and helps exclude infection and malignancy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A bronchoscopy is performed under light sedation; a small amount of saline is instilled into a lung segment and recovered by suction. The procedure takes 20–30 minutes and requires recovery time. The BAL cell differential is reported within 24–48 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms the causal relationship between cork dust exposure and the lung reaction by reproducing symptoms and physiological changes under controlled conditions; used in diagnostically uncertain cases.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed in a specialist lung function laboratory; the patient inhales a standardised preparation of the suspected antigen and is monitored for lung function changes and symptoms over 8–24 hours. Requires hospital admission or close outpatient observation.

diagnosticTests.test: Surgical lung biopsy (VATS or open biopsy)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Provides definitive histological diagnosis when non-invasive tests are inconclusive; the typical pattern shows poorly formed non-necrotising granulomas, lymphocytic alveolitis, and peribronchiolar inflammation.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed under general anaesthesia; video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery (VATS) is the standard approach. Hospital stay of 1–3 days. Reserved for cases where the diagnosis is uncertain and it would materially change management.

treatmentOverview: The foundation of suberosis treatment is complete cessation of exposure to cork dust, which is the only intervention proven to prevent progression to fibrosis. In acute episodes, removing the worker from the environment is often sufficient for symptom resolution within 24–72 hours. For moderate to severe acute or subacute disease, a course of oral corticosteroids — typically prednisolone 40–60 mg daily, tapered over 8–12 weeks — reduces alveolar inflammation and accelerates functional recovery, though it does not alter long-term prognosis once fibrosis is established. The 2020 ATS/ERS/JRS/ALAT clinical practice guidelines for hypersensitivity pneumonitis recommend antigen avoidance as the primary intervention, with corticosteroids reserved for significant or progressive disease. For workers who cannot immediately leave cork processing employment, strict engineering controls — industrial-grade dust extraction, enclosed and pressurised respiratory protective equipment, and job rotation away from highest-dust tasks — can reduce exposure sufficiently to limit further sensitisation. Oxygen therapy is indicated for hypoxaemic patients. In advanced fibrosis with end-stage respiratory failure, lung transplantation is the only curative option, but the threshold for transplant referral requires documented disease progression despite optimal antigen avoidance. Monitoring of lung function every 6–12 months allows early detection of progression. Management of suberosis should involve coordination between the pulmonologist, occupational health physician, and the patient's employer to optimise both medical treatment and workplace exposure controls.

medicalTreatments.name: Antigen avoidance (occupational exposure cessation)
medicalTreatments.description: Complete removal from cork dust exposure is the single most effective intervention for preventing disease progression. When antigen avoidance is achieved early, before fibrosis develops, lung function typically stabilises or partially recovers. Even after fibrosis is established, continued exposure accelerates decline.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: In patients with acute or subacute suberosis, complete exposure cessation leads to symptomatic improvement in approximately 70–80% of cases within weeks to months; fibrotic disease stabilises but does not reverse in most patients.

medicalTreatments.name: Prednisolone (oral corticosteroids)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line pharmacological treatment for moderate to severe acute or subacute suberosis; typically prednisolone 40–60 mg daily for 4 weeks, then tapered over 8–12 weeks. Reduces alveolar inflammation and improves DLCO and FVC during the acute phase. Long-term use carries standard corticosteroid side effects.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Corticosteroids achieve more rapid short-term improvement in lung function — approximately 15–25% improvement in FVC within 4 weeks — than antigen avoidance alone in acute disease, though long-term outcomes are determined primarily by whether antigen avoidance is achieved.

medicalTreatments.name: Inhaled corticosteroids (adjunct)
medicalTreatments.description: Used in some patients with persistent airway inflammation after the acute phase resolves, or in those with coexisting occupational asthma; less evidence-based for hypersensitivity pneumonitis specifically than systemic corticosteroids but reduces inhalational burden of systemic therapy in mild cases.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Limited controlled data for this indication; may offer approximately 10–15% improvement in airway symptoms in patients with concurrent bronchospasm component.

medicalTreatments.name: Supplemental oxygen therapy
medicalTreatments.description: Long-term oxygen therapy is indicated when resting arterial oxygen partial pressure falls below 55 mmHg (or below 60 mmHg with cor pulmonale or polycythaemia), following standard chronic respiratory failure criteria. Improves exercise capacity, quality of life, and survival in hypoxaemic patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Long-term oxygen therapy improves survival in hypoxaemic chronic respiratory failure by approximately 35–40% at 2 years compared with no oxygen, based on landmark NOTT and MRC trials.

medicalTreatments.name: Pulmonary rehabilitation
medicalTreatments.description: A supervised programme of exercise training, education, and psychological support for patients with chronic suberosis; improves exercise capacity, dyspnoea scores, and quality of life independent of lung function improvement.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Pulmonary rehabilitation improves the 6-minute walk distance by approximately 40–70 metres and reduces exertional dyspnoea scores significantly in chronic interstitial lung disease patients, with effects maintained for 6–12 months after the programme.

surgicalOptions.name: Lung transplantation (bilateral or single)
surgicalOptions.description: Reserved for patients with end-stage suberosis-related fibrosis and respiratory failure who have progressed despite optimal antigen avoidance and medical management; bilateral lung transplantation is preferred for hypersensitivity pneumonitis-related fibrosis. Referral should occur when FVC falls below 60% predicted or DLCO below 40% with continued decline.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 5-year survival after lung transplantation for interstitial lung disease averages approximately 50–55% in large registry data; outcomes are best in younger, otherwise healthy recipients at experienced transplant centres.

alternativeTreatments.name: Engineering dust controls and industrial ventilation
alternativeTreatments.description: For workers who remain in cork industry employment, fitted N95 or PAPR respirators, powered air-purifying equipment, wet suppression of cork dust, local exhaust ventilation, and job rotation to lower-exposure tasks collectively reduce antigen inhalation dose. These are occupational health interventions rather than medical treatments but are the primary preventive approach for the entire exposed workforce.

alternativeTreatments.name: Pulmonary rehabilitation exercise programme
alternativeTreatments.description: Non-pharmacological supervised exercise training improves breathlessness, exercise tolerance, and quality of life in established suberosis with fibrosis; validated as a component of management for hypersensitivity pneumonitis and all chronic interstitial lung diseases.

whySeeSpecialist: Suberosis should be managed by a respiratory specialist (pulmonologist) experienced in interstitial lung diseases and hypersensitivity pneumonitis. The diagnosis requires specialist lung function testing, HRCT interpretation, antigen serology, and in uncertain cases bronchoscopy or surgical biopsy. An occupational medicine physician should be involved to assess the workplace, advise on permissible exposure levels, arrange alternative duties, and liaise with occupational health services and the employer. Early specialist involvement is critical because the window to prevent fibrosis closes as cumulative exposure continues.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a pulmonologist with a specialist interest in interstitial lung diseases and occupational lung disorders, ideally working in a centre with access to HRCT, DLCO measurement, bronchoscopy, and serum precipitin testing. Occupational medicine input is essential and can often be arranged through the national labour health authority in cork-producing countries. For end-stage disease, a lung transplant programme should be involved early in the decision-making process.
preventionStrategies: Implement engineering dust controls at source — industrial ventilation, local exhaust extraction, and enclosure of the dustiest cork grinding and cutting operations.
Provide and enforce the use of fitted N95 or powered air-purifying respirators (PAPR) for all workers in high-dust areas.
Conduct regular occupational health surveillance — annual spirometry and DLCO measurement — for all cork industry workers to detect sensitisation and early disease before fibrosis develops.
Educate workers about the typical symptom pattern of suberosis (flu-like illness after work exposure, improving at weekends) so that early cases are recognised and reported.
Immediately reduce or eliminate exposure for any worker with confirmed suberosis or rising serum precipitins with symptoms.
Store cork bark in dry, well-ventilated conditions to minimise mould colonisation and spore production before processing.
lifestyleModifications: If suberosis is confirmed, prioritise removal from high-exposure cork dust environments as the single most important step — even if it requires a job transfer or retraining.
Stop smoking: smoking worsens the functional impact of established fibrosis, increases cardiovascular risk, and complicates management.
Maintain physical activity appropriate to current lung capacity; deconditioning worsens dyspnoea independently of lung function decline.
Learn to pace activities and conserve energy: breaking tasks into smaller steps and planning rest periods reduces the impact of exertional dyspnoea on daily life.
Seek occupational health advice about legal entitlements — in Portugal and Spain, suberosis is a recognised occupational disease with provisions for compensation and job redeployment.

recommended: A high-protein, balanced diet to support respiratory muscle strength and immune function
Adequate calcium and vitamin D intake, especially important in patients on long-term corticosteroids to protect bone density
Anti-inflammatory foods rich in omega-3 fatty acids (oily fish, walnuts) and antioxidants, which may modestly support pulmonary health
avoid: Excess sodium, which worsens fluid retention in patients on corticosteroids or in those with cor pulmonale
Alcohol, which impairs mucociliary clearance, reduces respiratory muscle function, and interacts adversely with some immunosuppressants
Very large meals, which distend the abdomen and can worsen dyspnoea in patients with advanced lung disease by mechanically limiting diaphragm descent

exerciseGuidelines: Regular aerobic exercise is strongly encouraged in proportion to current lung capacity, guided by a pulmonary rehabilitation programme. Exercise improves breathing efficiency, cardiovascular fitness, and quality of life without worsening the underlying lung disease. Target breathlessness levels on the Borg scale of 3–5 (moderate) during exercise are appropriate. Walking, cycling, and swimming are well tolerated. Patients who desaturate below 90% during exercise should use supplemental portable oxygen during activity. Strength training of the upper limb and respiratory muscles complements aerobic exercise in pulmonary rehabilitation.
dailyManagement: If still working in the cork industry, wear the prescribed respirator at all times in dusty areas; never remove it during grinding, boiling, or cutting operations.
Take any prescribed medications consistently, including completing tapering corticosteroid courses as directed without stopping abruptly.
Monitor for signs of corticosteroid side effects (glucose rise, blood pressure increase, weight gain) if on prednisolone and report them to the treating physician.
Keep a symptom and exercise diary to detect any deterioration in exercise tolerance that should prompt earlier specialist review.
Attend all scheduled pulmonary function tests and HRCT monitoring appointments to track disease progression and guide management decisions.
prognosis: The prognosis of suberosis depends almost entirely on how early exposure is reduced and whether fibrosis has developed at the time of diagnosis. In acute and subacute disease without established fibrosis, complete exposure avoidance leads to symptomatic improvement in 70–80% of workers and stabilisation of lung function in the majority. A subset — estimated at 20–30% of those with subacute disease — will develop progressive fibrosis despite antigen avoidance and corticosteroid treatment, similar to the fibrotic progression seen in other hypersensitivity pneumonitides. Once honeycombing is visible on CT, lung function decline is often relentless and follows a trajectory similar to idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis. Five-year mortality from advanced fibrotic suberosis is comparable to other fibrotic interstitial lung diseases, reported in the range of 30–50% in historical series. Early diagnosis, aggressive exposure control, and regular surveillance represent the best strategy to maintain long-term lung health in exposed workers.
recoveryTimeline: Acute suberosis typically resolves within 24–72 hours of leaving the exposure environment, with full return to baseline in 1–4 weeks. Subacute disease improves over 2–4 months after exposure cessation and corticosteroid treatment. Lung function returns to near-normal in 6–12 months in cases without fibrosis. In fibrotic disease, stabilisation rather than reversal is the realistic target — decline in FVC of less than 5% per year is considered a satisfactory response to treatment. Patients with advanced fibrosis who progress to end-stage respiratory failure may require lung transplantation, with post-transplant recovery spanning 6–12 months of intensive rehabilitation.
complications: Irreversible pulmonary fibrosis — the most significant complication; develops in 20–30% of patients with subacute disease despite antigen avoidance, causing progressive restrictive lung failure.
Chronic hypoxaemia and cor pulmonale — pulmonary arterial hypertension from hypoxic vasoconstriction in advanced fibrosis leading to right heart failure, ankle oedema, and reduced exercise capacity.
Recurrent acute episodes — each episode of alveolitis adds to cumulative lung injury; workers who return to exposure without adequate protection repeatedly sustain damage that accelerates fibrosis.

Opportunistic infections — patients on long-term oral corticosteroids are at increased risk for Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia and other opportunistic infections; prophylaxis should be considered in those requiring prolonged high-dose therapy.

Corticosteroid-related complications — prolonged prednisolone therapy causes osteoporosis, glucose intolerance, hypertension, and adrenal suppression that require monitoring and preventive management.
End-stage respiratory failure — in advanced fibrosis, respiratory failure may require long-term oxygen therapy and eventual lung transplant assessment.

supportResources.name: NHS — Extrinsic Allergic Alveolitis
supportResources.description: UK NHS patient guidance on hypersensitivity pneumonitis covering causes, symptoms, and management.

supportResources.name: ERS — European Respiratory Society Patient Resources
supportResources.description: European Respiratory Society patient and public education resources on interstitial lung diseases and occupational lung conditions.

sources.title: Lacasse Y et al. Clinical diagnosis of hypersensitivity pneumonitis. American Journal of Respiratory and Critical Care Medicine, 2003
Fernandez Alvarez R et al. Cork worker's lung (suberosis). Medicina Clinica (Barcelona), 2004
Morell F et al. Suberosis (cork dust hypersensitivity pneumonitis). Archivos de Bronconeumología, 1992

confusedWithConditions.name: Farmer's lung
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Farmer's lung is hypersensitivity pneumonitis caused by Saccharopolyspora rectivirgula spores from mouldy hay or grain; the mechanism and lung pathology are identical to suberosis but the antigen source and occupational setting are different. Distinguishing them requires specific antigen serology.

confusedWithConditions.name: Pulmonary sarcoidosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Sarcoidosis causes non-caseating granulomas on biopsy, typically affects mediastinal lymph nodes, and shows upper-lobe nodules on CT in a perilymphatic distribution; unlike suberosis it is not occupationally triggered and has a different antigen serology profile.

confusedWithConditions.name: Asthma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Asthma causes reversible airway obstruction with wheeze and is driven by a type I (IgE-mediated) allergic mechanism; suberosis produces a restrictive pattern on spirometry with alveolar inflammation rather than bronchospasm, and is not relieved by short-acting bronchodilators.

confusedWithConditions.name: Occupational asthma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Occupational asthma from inhaled cork dust causes airway obstruction with wheeze, reversible with bronchodilators; suberosis causes alveolar inflammation and fibrosis with a restrictive pattern. Both can coexist, but the diagnostic approach and treatment differ.

relatedConditions.name: Farmer's lung
relatedConditions.relevance: The most common and best-studied form of hypersensitivity pneumonitis; same mechanism and pathology as suberosis but triggered by mould spores from mouldy hay and agricultural dust rather than cork.

relatedConditions.name: Pulmonary sarcoidosis
relatedConditions.relevance: A granulomatous interstitial lung disease that can closely mimic suberosis on imaging and biopsy; distinguishing the two requires occupational history, antigen serology, and careful interpretation of the granuloma pattern.

relatedConditions.name: Asbestosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another occupational lung disease causing progressive interstitial fibrosis from inhaled particles, an important differential in the evaluation of occupational-exposure pulmonary fibrosis.

relatedConditions.name: COPD
relatedConditions.relevance: Often coexists with suberosis in older workers with smoking history; the obstructive and restrictive patterns can overlap and complicate functional assessment.

faqs.question: What is suberosis?
faqs.answer: Suberosis is an occupational lung disease caused by inhaling fungal spores — mainly Penicillium glabrum — from cork dust in the cork processing industry. It is a form of hypersensitivity pneumonitis that causes alveolar inflammation and, without exposure reduction, can progress to irreversible lung scarring.

faqs.question: Who gets suberosis?
faqs.answer: Suberosis primarily affects workers in the cork processing industry in Portugal, Spain, Algeria, and Morocco. Stripping, grinding, boiling, and cutting cork bark generate high concentrations of fungal spores. About 8–15% of heavily exposed workers develop the condition.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of suberosis?
faqs.answer: Typical symptoms include breathlessness on exertion, persistent dry cough, and — in acute episodes — fever, chills, and muscle aching starting 4–8 hours after entering the cork plant and resolving after leaving. Chronic disease adds progressive breathlessness and fatigue.

faqs.question: Is suberosis curable?
faqs.answer: Suberosis can be controlled and, if caught early before fibrosis develops, lung function can stabilise or recover significantly with complete exposure avoidance. Once interstitial fibrosis is established, it is not reversible, though progression can be slowed with antigen avoidance and sometimes corticosteroids.

faqs.question: How is suberosis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis combines an occupational history of cork dust exposure, spirometry showing a restrictive pattern and reduced diffusing capacity, a high-resolution CT of the chest, and blood tests for IgG antibodies against Penicillium glabrum and Chrysonilia sitophila antigens. Bronchoscopy with lavage or lung biopsy is used in uncertain cases.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for suberosis?
faqs.answer: The primary treatment is removing the worker from cork dust exposure. For significant or progressive disease, a tapering course of oral prednisolone over 8–12 weeks reduces alveolar inflammation. Pulmonary rehabilitation, supplemental oxygen for hypoxaemia, and regular lung function monitoring are essential components of ongoing care.

faqs.question: Can suberosis kill you?
faqs.answer: Advanced suberosis with irreversible pulmonary fibrosis and respiratory failure can be fatal. Five-year mortality in advanced fibrotic disease is estimated at 30–50%. Early diagnosis and prompt exposure cessation substantially reduce this risk. Most workers diagnosed before fibrosis develops have a much more favourable prognosis.

faqs.question: Is suberosis the same as farmer's lung?
faqs.answer: Suberosis and farmer's lung are both forms of hypersensitivity pneumonitis with identical immune mechanisms and lung pathology. The difference is the antigen source: suberosis is triggered by Penicillium glabrum from cork, while farmer's lung is triggered by Saccharopolyspora rectivirgula from mouldy hay.

faqs.question: What fungi cause suberosis?
faqs.answer: The main causative agents are Penicillium glabrum (formerly Penicillium frequentans) and Chrysonilia sitophila (formerly Neurospora sitophila), both of which colonise stored cork bark. Sensitised workers develop IgG antibodies against these organisms detectable in blood serology.

faqs.question: How does suberosis differ from asthma?
faqs.answer: Suberosis causes alveolar inflammation and a restrictive lung function pattern — reduced lung volumes and impaired gas transfer — rather than the reversible airway obstruction and wheeze of asthma. Suberosis is not relieved by inhalers and is diagnosed by different tests including HRCT and diffusing capacity measurement.

faqs.question: Can you still work in cork processing after getting suberosis?
faqs.answer: Continuing in high-exposure cork processing with suberosis risks progressive fibrosis. If complete job change is not possible, strict engineering controls — industrial ventilation, powered air-purifying respirators, and rotation away from highest-dust tasks — can reduce exposure enough to slow disease. Occupational health advice is essential.

faqs.question: Does wearing a mask prevent suberosis?
faqs.answer: A properly fitted N95 or powered air-purifying respirator reduces the inhaled spore dose substantially and is one of the most effective individual preventive measures. However, engineering controls such as dust extraction and ventilation at source are more reliably effective than relying on respirator use alone.

faqs.question: What does suberosis look like on a CT scan?
faqs.answer: In acute or subacute suberosis, high-resolution CT shows bilateral ground-glass opacification, centrilobular nodules, and areas of mosaic attenuation reflecting air trapping. Advanced fibrotic disease shows honeycombing, traction bronchiectasis, and volume loss predominantly in the upper and mid-lung zones.

faqs.question: Is suberosis recognised as an occupational disease for compensation?
faqs.answer: Yes. Suberosis is classified as an occupational disease in Portugal and Spain, the main cork-producing countries, and workers who develop it may be entitled to job redeployment, compensation, and medical support through occupational health legislation. A formal occupational medicine assessment documents the link between work and illness.

faqs.question: How often should cork workers be screened for suberosis?
faqs.answer: Occupational health guidelines recommend annual spirometry and diffusing capacity measurement (DLCO) for all workers with significant cork dust exposure. Workers with any respiratory symptoms or abnormal lung function results warrant more frequent monitoring and specialist referral.

faqs.question: Can suberosis come back after exposure has stopped?
faqs.answer: Once exposure ceases and the acute alveolitis resolves, the condition does not typically relapse unless the worker returns to cork dust contact. However, established fibrosis can continue to progress slowly even after complete antigen avoidance in a proportion of patients.

faqs.question: What is the role of steroids in suberosis?
faqs.answer: Oral prednisolone at 40–60 mg daily, tapered over 8–12 weeks, is used for moderate to severe acute or subacute suberosis to reduce alveolar inflammation and accelerate functional recovery. Steroids do not reverse established fibrosis and are used alongside — not instead of — antigen avoidance.

faqs.question: Why do only some cork workers get suberosis?
faqs.answer: Only 8–15% of heavily exposed workers develop suberosis despite similar exposures. Genetic factors — particularly certain HLA haplotypes — influence the immune response to inhaled fungal antigens, making some individuals more likely to develop sensitisation and disease than others.

faqs.question: Can suberosis lead to lung transplant?
faqs.answer: In advanced cases with end-stage fibrosis and respiratory failure that has progressed despite optimal treatment, lung transplantation is the only option. Transplant referral should occur when FVC falls below 60% predicted or DLCO below 40% with continued decline, before the patient is too ill to undergo surgery.

faqs.question: What is the link between smoking and suberosis?
faqs.answer: Active smoking paradoxically reduces the risk of developing hypersensitivity pneumonitis sensitisation — possibly through immune suppression of the alveolar response — but worsens the functional impact of established disease and adds its own risk of COPD and lung cancer. Smoking cessation is always recommended.

regionalNames.name: Pulmón de los trabajadores del corcho

metaDescription: Suberosis (cork worker's lung) is an occupational hypersensitivity pneumonitis from cork dust. Learn symptoms, diagnosis and treatment options. Doctor-reviewed.
